description_long: This guard stone is a robust, vertically oriented structure constructed primarily from large, weathered stone blocks arranged in an arch formation. The front features a prominent sculpted face or mask with geometric, angular details and a blue stone or tile set into the forehead, giving it a somewhat mystical or historical appearance. Moss and discoloration are visible on the stone surfaces, especially in recessed areas, suggesting prolonged exposure to the elements. The base is circular and slightly raised, with a metal ring embedded below the sculpted face, possibly for tethering or decorative purposes. The rear is flat and less detailed, covered with patches of moss and weather stains. The overall design evokes a sense of ancient or ceremonial urban infrastructure, blending both functional and ornamental qualities.
description: A tall, arched stone guard stone with a sculpted face, blue tile, and mossy weathered surfaces, featuring a metal ring below the face.
description_view_0: Front view shows the sculpted face, blue tile, metal ring, and pronounced mossy stonework.
description_view_1: Side view reveals the depth of the face sculpture and the layered stone construction.
description_view_2: Rear view displays a flat, moss-covered stone surface with no sculptural details.
description_view_3: Opposite side view highlights the protruding face and the thickness of the stone arch.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the entrance to driveways, alleyways, or along the corners of urban streets, often near historical sites or older neighborhoods to protect structures from vehicle impact.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, standing upright and possibly anchored into a stone or concrete base for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible moss growth and discoloration on lower and recessed surfaces, likely from rainwater runoff and shade. Some smoothing and minor chipping on protruding edges, probably from incidental contact with vehicles or foot traffic. Weathering is most pronounced on the front and base.
